ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bubble tea class; no specific honey-lemon variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no dedicated bubble tea or honey-lemon tea sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for honey lemon bubble tea identified in the public SNOMED CT browser.

preparation_method: served chilled or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of honey lemon tea bubble tea is one medium takeaway cup of approximately 500 ml, which includes the tea base, honey-lemon syrup, and a full portion of tapioca pearls.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Lemon Tea (Bubble Tea) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D08010051 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are preserved from the HPB source. Protein and fat are reported as 0.0 g/100 g by HPB for this beverage formulation.

gi_source.title: Tapioca and sugar-sweetened tea beverage GI — analogous estimate based on tapioca pearls (GI ~70) and sugar-sweetened beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for honey lemon tea bubble tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from the closest analogous foods: tapioca pearls (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database) and sugar-sweetened tea beverages (GI ~65–72). The dominant carbohydrate contributors are sucrose/honey (GI ~65) and tapioca starch (GI ~70). A blended GI of 70 is defensible and consistent with the input record. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 62.5) / 100 = 43.75 for a 500 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (12.34 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 evidence, despite low sodium (10 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid honey lemon tea bubble tea; the 12.34 g/100 g sugar load in liquid form is an independent blood pressure risk factor and a single 500 ml serving delivers approximately 62 g of sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 12.34 g sugar/100 g and high-GI tapioca pearls (GI ~70) produces rapid postprandial glucose elevation with minimal fibre buffering (0.29 g/100 g).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Honey lemon tea bubble tea should be avoided by individuals managing type 2 diabetes; a standard 500 ml serving delivers approximately 62 g of rapidly absorbed sugars and high-GI tapioca starch, causing sharp postprandial glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar content (12.34 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased CVD risk, and contributes to excess caloric intake without nutritional benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid honey lemon tea bubble tea due to its high free-sugar content, which is linked to dyslipidaemia and increased cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (12.34 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (0.29 g/100 g) and high-GI tapioca pearls promotes pro-inflammatory mediators with no anti-inflammatory nutritional offset.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid honey lemon tea bubble tea; its high sugar and refined starch content promotes systemic inflammation and provides no anti-inflammatory nutritional benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey lemon tea bubble tea is a popular sweetened cold beverage sold widely at bubble tea shops across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It is prepared by brewing black or green tea, then blending it with honey, fresh or concentrated lemon juice, and sugar syrup, served over ice or at room temperature with cooked tapioca pearls (also called boba or pearls) added at the bottom of the cup. The drink has a sweet-tart flavour profile with floral honey notes and a refreshing citrus brightness from the lemon. The tapioca pearls contribute a distinctive chewy, gummy texture that contrasts with the liquid tea base. Per 100 g, the beverage provides 50 kcal, 12.5 g carbohydrate (of which 12.34 g is sugar), 0.29 g dietary fibre, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, and 10 mg sodium. A standard 500 ml serving delivers approximately 250 kcal and 62 g of sugar, making it a high-sugar beverage. The glycemic index is estimated at 70 (high), driven by the sucrose and honey content and the high-GI tapioca starch. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high free-sugar load. It is also pro-inflammatory and should be avoided in rheumatoid arthritis management. Despite its low sodium content, the sugar-sweetened beverage classification independently raises blood pressure risk. The drink is vegetarian, dairy-free, and halal-suitable. It is a culturally ubiquitous beverage in Singapore, commonly consumed as a snack or refreshment throughout the day.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010051, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). This is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. All core macros are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using tapioca pearls and sugar-sweetened tea as reference foods.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — this is a carbohydrate-dominant sweetened tea beverage containing no protein-contributing ingredients at measurable levels. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — the beverage contains no fat-contributing ingredients (no milk, cream, or oil). saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 consistent with fat_g = 0.0 confirmed zero. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific honey lemon tea bubble tea formulation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D08010051. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using tapioca pearls (GI ~70) and sugar-sweetened tea beverages as reference foods; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the database. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 500 g standard cup: GL = (70 × 62.5) / 100 = 43.75. The vegan flag is set to false because honey is an animal-derived product. Ontology codes use closest defensible matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code for honey lemon bubble tea exists in public catalogues as of 2026.
